title: Intro to Linked Lists: Draw Your Data
subtitle: Learn how linked lists work by drawing nodes and arrows on paper.
encouragement: Linked lists are one of the most elegant data structures. Each piece of data knows where the next one lives. Once you draw it, the logic clicks. Let's go.
realWorldExample: Think of a scavenger hunt: each clue tells you where the next clue is. You can't skip ahead; you follow the chain. Music playlists work the same way: each song points to the next one to play.

materials.detail: Landscape orientation. This is your workspace where you'll draw the linked list.
You'll draw nodes in pencil so you can erase and redraw when inserting or deleting.
For drawing the "next" pointer arrows between nodes.
Write A, B, C, D, and E on separate small cards. These represent your data values.

setup: Place your large sheet of paper in landscape orientation in front of you.
Prepare 5 small paper cards labeled A, B, C, D, and E. Set them aside for now.
Have your pencil, eraser, and blue pen ready.
Leave plenty of space between where you'll draw nodes. You'll need room to insert new ones later.

steps.instruction: Draw your first node. A node is a rectangle split into two halves: the left half holds data, the right half holds a next pointer.
steps.action: With your pencil, draw a rectangle near the left side of the paper. Draw a vertical line in the middle to split it into two sections. Place the "A" card in the left section. Leave the right section empty for now.
steps.checkpoint: You should have one rectangle with two sections: [A | ].
steps.conceptNote: In code, a node is an object with two fields: data and next. Right now, next is None because this node doesn't point anywhere yet.
steps.hint: Make each rectangle about 2 inches wide so you have room for the card and the arrow.

steps.instruction: Draw three more nodes to the right of the first one: B, C, and D. Space them out evenly.
steps.action: Draw three more split rectangles to the right. Place B, C, and D cards in the left halves. Leave the right halves empty.
steps.checkpoint: You should have four separate rectangles in a row: [A | ] [B | ] [C | ] [D | ].
steps.hint: Leave space between each node. You'll draw arrows in this gap.

steps.instruction: Now link them together. Pick up your blue pen. Draw an arrow from A's right section to B's left edge.
steps.action: In blue, draw an arrow starting from the right half of A's box, pointing to B. Then draw an arrow from B to C, and from C to D. In D's right half, draw a small slash or write "null" to show it points nowhere.
steps.checkpoint: You should see: [A | ->] -> [B | ->] -> [C | ->] -> [D | null]. All arrows are in blue.
steps.conceptNote: Each blue arrow is the next pointer. Node A's next points to B. Node D's next is None, marking the end of the list.

steps.instruction: Write "head" above node A with a small downward arrow. This is the entry point to the list.
steps.action: In pencil, write "head" above node A with an arrow pointing down to it.
steps.checkpoint: The word "head" should be above node A. This is the only way into the list.
steps.conceptNote: A linked list stores a reference called head that points to the first node. Without it, you can't access any node in the list.

steps.instruction: Let's traverse. Put your finger on the head node (A). Now follow each blue arrow to the next node. Say each letter out loud as you touch it.
steps.action: Touch A, say "A". Follow the blue arrow to B, say "B". Follow to C, say "C". Follow to D, say "D". D's next is null, so you stop.
steps.checkpoint: You should have said: A, B, C, D. You visited every node by following arrows, one at a time.
steps.conceptNote: Traversal visits each node exactly once by following next pointers. In code: current = head, then current = current.next in a loop until current is None.
steps.hint: Notice you can't jump directly to C. You MUST start at head and follow the chain. This is different from an array where you can access any index directly.

steps.instruction: Time to insert a new node. We'll add E between B and C. First, draw a new node for E below the gap between B and C.
steps.action: Draw a new split rectangle below and between B and C. Place the "E" card in the left half.
steps.checkpoint: You should have a new node [E | ] floating below the main chain, between B and C.
steps.hint: Drawing it below first helps you see the rewiring before you move it into place.

steps.instruction: Now rewire. Erase the blue arrow from B to C.
steps.action: Use your eraser to remove the blue arrow that goes from B's right half to C. B's right half is now empty.
steps.checkpoint: The chain is broken: A -> B [no arrow] ... C -> D. Node E is still floating below, unconnected.
steps.conceptNote: This is the key insight: inserting into a linked list means changing pointers, not moving data around. In an array, you'd have to shift C and D to make room.

steps.instruction: Draw two new blue arrows: one from B to E, and one from E to C.
steps.action: With your blue pen, draw an arrow from B's right half to E. Then draw an arrow from E's right half to C.
steps.checkpoint: The chain should now read: A -> B -> E -> C -> D. Traverse with your finger to verify.
steps.conceptNote: Insertion took only 2 pointer changes, no matter how long the list is. This is O(1) once you have a reference to the node before the insertion point.
steps.hint: Traverse the whole list with your finger to confirm: A, B, E, C, D.

steps.instruction: Now let's delete node C from the list. First, find C by traversing from head.
steps.action: Put your finger on A. Follow arrows: A -> B -> E -> C. Found it. Now erase the blue arrow from E to C. Erase the blue arrow from C to D. Draw a new blue arrow from E directly to D.
steps.checkpoint: The chain should now read: A -> B -> E -> D. Node C is disconnected and can be removed from the paper.
steps.conceptNote: Deletion also requires only pointer changes. We redirected E's next to skip over C and point to D. In code: previous.next = current.next.
steps.hint: Remove the C card and erase its rectangle. In a program, the garbage collector reclaims this memory.

steps.instruction: Final check. Traverse your finished list one more time. How many pointer changes did insertion take? How many did deletion take?
steps.action: Traverse: A -> B -> E -> D -> null. Insertion changed 2 pointers (B's next and E's next). Deletion changed 1 pointer (E's next to skip C).
steps.checkpoint: Your final list is A -> B -> E -> D with head pointing to A.
steps.conceptNote: Linked lists excel at insertion and deletion because they only rewire pointers. Arrays excel at random access. Each structure has its strengths.

reflection.question: What is the difference between accessing element C in an array vs. in a linked list?
reflection.answer: In an array, you jump directly to index 2. In a linked list, you must start at head and follow arrows one by one until you reach C. Arrays give O(1) access; linked lists give O(n).

reflection.question: Why is inserting in the middle faster for a linked list than an array?
reflection.answer: A linked list just redraws 2 arrows (pointer changes). An array must shift every element after the insertion point to make room.

reflection.question: What happens if you lose the head pointer?
reflection.answer: You lose access to the entire list. Head is the only entry point. Without it, there's no way to reach any node.

reflection.question: Could you traverse the list backward from D to A?
reflection.answer: No. Each node only has a "next" pointer going forward. To go backward, you'd need a doubly linked list with "prev" pointers as well.
